coredata.dc:description: State-of-the-art enhanced condensation techniques demonstrate inferior filmwise thermal performance and long-term reliability in the case of extremely wetting low surface tension fluids. To address this issue, the converging wick structure concept is proposed for enhanced flow condensation of low surface-tension fluids. The converging wick structure offers promising features, including strategic distribution of the solid-fluid interfacial area along the condenser length to account for the variations in the local vapor quality. It also considers a converging profile to compensate for the reduced convective effects due to the vapor-to-liquid transition. Additionally, the 3D-printed copper wick structures are mechanically robust and in their intrinsic state (i.e., no coating or liquid infusion), thereby offering long-term reliability. A semi-analytical wick flow condensation model for low surface-tension fluids is also developed to predict the thermal performance of the proposed converging wick flow condenser and offer guidelines for future optimization efforts. The wick flow condenser is 3D-printed with GRCop-42, a high-thermal-conductivity copper alloy. The experimental and modeling results show that the proposed converging wick flow condenser offers a higher overall condensation performance compared with its plain condenser counterpart. Insights gained from the current study offer new pathways to engineer compact and durable 3D-printed wick flow condensers with promising heat transfer characteristics for low surface-tension fluids.

head.ce:title: 3D-printed converging wick flow condensers for low surface-tension fluids: Modeling and experiment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: State-of-the-art enhanced condensation techniques demonstrate inferior filmwise thermal performance and long-term reliability in the case of extremely wetting low surface tension fluids. To address this issue, the converging wick structure concept is proposed for enhanced flow condensation of low surface-tension fluids. The converging wick structure offers promising features, including strategic distribution of the solid-fluid interfacial area along the condenser length to account for the variations in the local vapor quality. It also considers a converging profile to compensate for the reduced convective effects due to the vapor-to-liquid transition. Additionally, the 3D-printed copper wick structures are mechanically robust and in their intrinsic state (i.e., no coating or liquid infusion), thereby offering long-term reliability. A semi-analytical wick flow condensation model for low surface-tension fluids is also developed to predict the thermal performance of the proposed converging wick flow condenser and offer guidelines for future optimization efforts. The wick flow condenser is 3D-printed with GRCop-42, a high-thermal-conductivity copper alloy. The experimental and modeling results show that the proposed converging wick flow condenser offers a higher overall condensation performance compared with its plain condenser counterpart. Insights gained from the current study offer new pathways to engineer compact and durable 3D-printed wick flow condensers with promising heat transfer characteristics for low surface-tension fluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Condensation heat transfer is a key process prevalent in myriad industrial applications, including electronic thermal management systems [1], air conditioning and heat pump systems [2], electric power generation [3], refrigeration [4], desalination [5], water harvesting [6], and the petrochemical industries [7], among others. Enhancing the performance of the condensation heat transfer process reduces the size, weight, power consumption, and cost (SWaP-C) of thermal management hardware. Extensive research has been conducted to promote dropwise condensation, demonstrating augmented heat transfer rates, using various techniques, including but not limited to hydrophobic coated surfaces [8], hydrophobic-hydrophilic hybrid coated surfaces [9], and slippery liquid-infused porous surfaces [10]. The majority of the dropwise-enhanced condensation techniques have been employed for external condensation [11]. However, there have been few efforts to apply hydrophobic/hydrophilic surfaces and micro and nanostructures for enhanced internal flow condensations [12–14]. These studies have found that the dropwise-enhanced condensation techniques inside channels demonstrate much lower improvement compared with external condensation under full condensation states (20–66 % [12–14] versus nearly one order of magnitude [11,15]). Additionally, while these techniques have been effective for enhanced dropwise condensation of high surface tension fluids, their functionalities are significantly hampered for low surface tension fluids (< 15 mN/m) such as fluorinated dielectrics and refrigerants, owing to their non-polar and extremely wetting behaviors, leading to wetting of both hydrophobic and hydrophilic condenser surfaces [16]. Although a recent study [17] has attempted to address this limitation by developing dropwise external condensation strategies for completely wetting refrigerants.

Among common approaches to enhanced condensation of low surface tension fluids are micro- and nono-textured surfaces/tubes [18] as they offer extended heat transfer areas and improved wettability and capillary effects in short length scales. Utilizing a hydrochloric acid-based etching and chemical oxidation approach, Mendizabal et al. [12] applied micro- and nano-structured surfaces on aluminum and copper tubes to examine in-tube condensation of the R1233zd(E) refrigerant. The etched micro-structure aluminum tube showed a 66 % improvement in heat transfer coefficient (HTC) compared with a smooth aluminum tube for a complete condensation state. However, the etched nano-structure copper oxide tube showed no significant change in thermal performance in comparison to its smooth copper counterpart. The authors concluded that this is mainly owing to the nano-scale structure of the copper oxide tube, which exhibits lower wettability and less capability of film modification. Torresin et al. [19] experimentally examined flow condensation on a copper-based nano-textured superhydrophobic surface. The superhydrophobic copper sample was prepared in a two-step process. First, a polished copper surface was immersed in a solution containing sodium hydroxide and ammonium persulfate. Then, the nanostructured surface was immersed in an ethanol solution of 1H,1H,2H,2H perfluorodecanethiol. Experimental results demonstrated a heat flux of about 600 kW/m2 at a subcooling of 10 °C. Even though the nano-textured superhydrophobic copper sample initially showed a promising condensation heat transfer rate, the hydrophobic coating sustained dropwise condensation for only five days, owing to mechanical degradation, resulting in a transition to filmwise condensation (FWC) manifested with a drop in thermal performance. A novel trapezoid drainage design was proposed by Chu et al. [20] to enhance the condensation heat transfer of dielectric fluid HFE-7100 in a micro-channel heat sink. Utilizing the drainage design, the condensate was effectively pulled into the drainage channels, resulting in a reduction in the liquid film thickness and an improvement in the two-phase heat transfer performance. The experimental results indicate that the trapezoid drainage design improves the two-phase HTC by 10–26 % compared to conventional rectangular micro-channel heat sinks. Although micro- and nano-textured and coated surfaces/tubes [21–25] have shown improvements in condensation heat transfer of low surface tension fluids; however, they are subject to several limitations hindering their broader applicability and long-term performance. This includes restrictions on fabricating 3D or custom-made features due to the nature of the manufacturing technique employed, such as chemical etching or coating. Also, coated surfaces suffer from longevity issues under liquid shearing and thermal cycling of the condensation process, resulting in a rapid decline in the heat transfer performance.

Alternatively, engineered topologies, including pin-fin tubes [26–28], wick structures [29–35], and micro-fin tubes [36–39] have been employed to improve the condensation heat transfer process of low surface tension fluids. Li et al. [39] compared the condensation heat transfer performances of a two-dimensional helix micro-fin tube and a three-dimensional dimple tube against a smooth tube utilizing R410A refrigerant as the working fluid. Compared with the smooth tube, the dimple and helix micro-fin tubes showed an improvement in the heat transfer coefficient by 1.15–2.05 and 1.18–1.69 times, respectively. Also, it was observed that increasing the thermal conductivity of tubes (from stainless steel to copper) had a small impact on the HTC of the smooth tube, while it had a more dramatic increase in the HTC of the enhanced tubes. Briggs [26] experimentally examined the condensation heat transfer of R113 and steam on six copper pin-fin tubes. The experimental data illustrated an enhancement in the vapor-side HTC by 3.6–9.9 times for the R113 and 2.4–2.9 times for the steam compared to a plain tube at a similar operating condition. A gravitationally driven copper foam wick condenser was proposed by Preston et al. [32] to eliminate the need for condensate-phobic coatings for low surface-tension fluids. Using pentane as a working fluid, a 350 % improvement in heat flux was experimentally demonstrated for the wick condenser compared to a plain surface. Employing a validated semi-analytical model, the authors showed an order-of-magnitude enhancement for the optimized metal wick, which could potentially outperform the traditional dropwise condensation. Although the aforementioned condenser topologies demonstrated enhanced condensation performance exceeding that of the smooth tubes and/or micro-channels [21–25], they are mainly fabricated using conventional fabrication approaches and, thus, are bound by their inherent constraints. These include limited flexibility in achieving fine feature sizes for enhanced condensation heat transfer rate and challenges in fabricating topologically complex or spatially varying structures, considering variations in the local vapor quality and directional capillarity during the condensation processes. Also, conventional fabrication methods often involve multi-step processes such as machining, etching, or bonding, which increase the complexity and cost associated with the fabrication of enhanced condenser technologies.

Advanced additive manufacturing techniques enable the realization of more intricate condenser designs, offering the potential to significantly augment condensation heat transfer beyond state-of-the-art condensers. Ho et al. [40] employed the selective laser melting (SLM) method to 3D print a three-dimensional pin-fin condenser. The experimental condensation results illustrated an improvement in the average HTC by about 100 % compared with a smooth surface. The condensation heat transfer process on sinusoidal and conical pin fins was experimentally investigated by Ho et al. [41,42]. For each sinusoidal and conical pin-fin design, nine surfaces with the same fin base diameter but different heights and pitches were 3D-printed utilizing the SLM method. The results showed that, as the fin height increased from 1.25 to 1.66 mm at a fixed fin pitch, the condensation heat transfer performances of both sinusoidal and conical pin-fin condensers improved. However, with a further increase in the fin height from 1.66 to 2.49 mm, a drop in the condensation heat flux and HTC was observed. Also, compared with a smooth surface, maximum thermal enhancement factors of 1.86 and 2.46 were achieved for sinusoidal and conical pin fins, respectively. Obviously, additive manufacturing can enable sophisticated heat transfer features for an enhanced condensation heat transfer process. However, the 3D-printed condenser technology is still in an early stage. Additive manufacturing of advanced condenser technologies currently faces several challenges, including limitations in achieving fine feature sizes with highly conductive materials for high condensation heat transfer coefficients, the need for support structures in complex geometries, and difficulties in removing residual powder from fine internal features. These limitations can restrict the effectiveness and scalability of additive-manufacturing-based designs for condensation applications. As such, there is a clear need to examine the potential of 3D printing in realizing advanced heat transfer topologies for next-generation enhanced 3D-printed condenser technologies.

In this study, 3D-printed copper-based flow condensers employing trapezoidal converging wick structures are proposed to enhance the condensation performance of low surface tension fluids. The thermo-hydraulic performance of the proposed converging wick flow condenser is examined under various operating conditions and benchmarked against a plain flow condenser. The wick and plain flow condensers are 3D-printed using the GRCop-42 copper alloy through the SLM method. In contrast to the above studies that employed additive manufacturing for condensation systems with relatively large feature sizes (on the order of several millimeters), the present work demonstrates the successful fabrication of fine features approximately 300 μm in size using a high thermal conductivity copper-based alloy to enhance the condensation heat transfer rate. Furthermore, the proposed wick structure is self-supporting and does not require internal supports, thereby reducing post-processing complexity and associated costs. Additionally, the pore size of the wick structure is designed to facilitate efficient powder removal during post-processing. The condensation experiments are conducted using HFE Novec-7100 as a representative low surface-tension working fluid. Additionally, a semi-analytical flow condensation model for low surface-tension fluids is developed to predict the thermal performance of the wick flow condenser at extended operating conditions. In the following, first, the conceptual design of the wick flow condenser is discussed in detail. Then, the 3D printing process, experimental test facility, and data reduction/uncertainty analysis are reviewed. Next, the wick flow condensation model is explained. Finally, the experimental and modeling results on the condensation thermo-hydraulic performance of the wick and plain flow condensers are presented.

State-of-the-art enhanced dropwise condensation techniques mainly rely on hydrophobic coatings with low contact angle hysteresis for effective droplet shedding. However, they demonstrate inferior performance and reliability in the case of popular low surface-tension fluids (e.g., dielectrics and refrigerants) due to their extremely wetting characteristics. Considering the short longevity of many existing hydrophobic-based coating methods and the high surface energy of commonly used metals, filmwise condensation is the dominant heat transfer mode in many industrial flow condensers employing low surface-tension fluids. Overcoming this limitation could reduce the size, weight, power consumption, and cost of thermal management hardware.

Here, a novel converging wick flow condenser is conceptualized to improve the condensation heat transfer rate of extremely low surface-tension fluids. As shown in Fig. 1 , the building block of the proposed flow condenser is a trapezoidal wick structure that converges along the flow direction. The converging wick structure offers several attractive features for enhanced flow condensation of low surface-tension fluids. First, the solid-fluid interfacial area along the condenser length is strategically distributed, considering the variation in the spatial distribution of the vapor quality. The thermal conductivity of the two-phase mixture is low at the condenser inlet due to the high vapor quality of the low-thermal-conductivity (low-k) vapor phase. The proposed converging wick flow condenser offers a high solid-fluid interfacial area at the condenser inlet to compensate for the low thermal conductivity of the two-phase mixture, thereby intensifying the condensation process in regions with high vapor qualities. Second, as the vapor phase transitions to the liquid phase along the flow direction, the convective momentum effects degrade due to an increase in the density of the two-phase mixture. The converging trapezoidal geometry aims to compensate for the reduced convective effects, thereby enhancing flow condensation in regions with low vapor qualities. Third, the presence of the vertically oriented side menisci (the circled region in Fig. 1) offers both additional liquid-vapor interfacial areas for enhanced vapor condensation and menisci for capillary wicking of the condensate from the heart of the wick structure to its outer periphery for drainage through the outlet wick section. The outlet wick for condensate drainage is also highlighted in Fig. 1. Additionally, individual wick structures are connected through the outlet wick to prevent a vapor breakthrough. The above features make the converging wick flow condenser highly effective in intensifying the condensation heat transfer rate of extremely wetting fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the converging wick flow condenser
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the converging wick flow condenser concept discussed in the previous section, a water-cooled wick flow condenser was designed. Fig. 2 a and 2b show the overall design and a cut view of the proposed wick flow condenser. The length, height, inlet width, and outlet width of the wick flow condenser are 102.8, 14.3, 43.6, and 13.7 mm, respectively. The condenser consists of a condensing dielectric and a chilled water side, separated by a thin wall of 1.6 mm in thickness. The condensing dielectric side employs two converging wick structures, each with maximum and minimum widths of 18.4 and 1 mm, respectively. Considering the copper 3D-printing limitations, a pore size and a wall thickness of 300 µm are considered. The chilled water side includes ten wavy channels, as shown in Fig. 2c, to improve the water-side heat transfer rate. The height and wall thickness of the wavy channels are 4.76 and 0.5 mm, respectively. The maximum inlet and minimum outlet spacing between wavy channels are 3.3 and 0.43 mm, respectively. The internal diameter of the inlet/outlet manifolds for both the dielectric and chilled water sides is 4.9 mm.

Additionally, a plain flow condenser with no heat transfer features on the dielectric side was 3D-printed as a baseline for thermal performance comparison (Fig. 2d). The primary reason for selecting a plain flow condenser as the baseline reference, rather than an enhanced structured flow condenser, is to establish a widely accepted and standardized plain flow condenser that facilitates benchmarking and allows for straightforward comparisons with other studies. It is worth noting that the wavy channels employed on the chilled water side are identical for both the wick and plain flow condensers. Additionally, the flow condensation experiments were conducted in a counter-flow configuration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Copper 3D printing of the converging wick flow condenser
ce:section.ce:para: Recent advancements in additive manufacturing techniques have enabled the realization of complex heat transfer features, surpassing the capabilities of conventional fabrication methods [43–45]. In this study, the proposed converging wick flow condenser and its plain counterpart were fabricated using the SLM method. To minimize the thermal resistance associated with the solid separating walls, a high thermal conductivity copper alloy material, GRCop-42, was employed. The GRCop-42 copper alloy (Carpenter Additive) consists primarily of copper (balance that brings the total composition to 100 wt %), with approximately 3.1–3.4 wt % chromium, 2.7–3.0 % niobium, and small amounts of aluminum (0.06 wt %), iron (0.025 wt %), silicon (0.035 wt %), and oxygen (0.04 wt %), providing a high thermal conductivity of about 344 W/m-K at room temperature [46]. Both wick and plain flow condensers were fabricated using an EOS M290 3D printer, which is one of the most advanced metal 3D printers currently available. The EOS M290 printer employs a high-power Yb-fiber laser of 400 W with a beam focus diameter of approximately 100 μm to 3D print highly detailed geometries. With this system and the GRCop-42 copper alloy, a minimum pore size of 50 μm and solid wall thicknesses of approximately 150 μm have been demonstrated in our group. However, to ensure proper connectivity of the laser-melted alloy pools, which is critical for achieving high thermal conductivity and mechanical strength, and to facilitate effective powder removal upon 3D printing, both the minimum pore size and minimum wall thickness were conservatively set to 300 μm in this study.

Fig. 3 shows the 3D-printed wick flow condenser made of the GRCop-42 alloy. Each condenser was 3D-printed integrally as one piece. As the proposed wick flow condenser utilizes thin features on the dielectric side, a proper 3D printing orientation needs to be considered for successful 3D printing. At a 3D printing angle of 30°, the 3D printing process started with the wider condenser side and then continued toward the narrower condenser side. The roughness measured on the 3D-printed GRCop-42 surfaces was about 20 µm. Also, stainless steel inlet/outlet tubes were brazed to the 3D-printed condensers for integration into a flow condensation test loop.

The powder removal process was conducted in two steps. In the first step, the 3D-printed wick condenser was submerged in an isopropyl alcohol (IPA) bath inside an ultrasonic cleaner for two hours. In this step, most of the powders trapped inside the wick structure were extracted. In the second step, IPA was pumped through the wick structures and wavy channels to remove any remaining powder inside the condenser modules. The two-step power removal process was repeated as necessary. The particle size distribution of the GRCop-42 powder employed in this study is shown in Fig. 4 . Considering both the volume and number of particle size distributions, almost all the GRCop-42 powders can pass through a pore size of 300 μm (i.e., the minimum pore size of the wick structure on the dielectric side).

A flow condensation test setup was developed to experimentally evaluate the condensation heat transfer performance of the converging wick flow condenser and its plain counterpart. Fig. 5 shows a schematic of the flow condensation test facility. In this study, a dielectric fluid, HFE Novec-7100, was utilized as a representative low surface-tension working fluid. Table 1 summarizes the properties of HFE-7100 in ambient conditions. To eliminate the negative impacts of the non-condensable gases (NCGs) on the condensation process, all NCGs were evacuated by a vacuum pump (Model: ScrollVac10+) prior to condensation experiments. A leakage test was conducted to ensure the dielectric loop was vacuum-proof. An ultrasonic mass flow meter with an accuracy of 1 % (Model: Keyence FD-XS1) was utilized to measure the mass flow rate of the HFE-7100 working fluid. The ultrasonic flow meter measures the volumetric flow rate. Since the fluid was subcooled (i.e., single phase) at the inlet of the flow meter, the density of the dielectric was known to calculate the mass flow rate. To ensure the accuracy of the ultrasonic flow meter, it was calibrated against a Coriolis flow meter (Model: Micro Motion ELITE CMF025H Coriolis Flow Meter, Emerson Electric Co., with an accuracy of 0.05 % RD) before the experiments. A custom-made electrical heater was employed to ensure HFE-7100 reached a 2∼3 °C superheat condition at the inlet of the condenser. Pressure and temperature of the dielectric fluid were measured at the inlet/outlet of the condenser utilizing pressure transducers with an accuracy of 0.1 % (Model: Setra 730 G) and T-type thermocouples with an accuracy of ±0.3 °C (Model: Omega TMTSS-062 U), respectively.

Additionally, the deionized (DI) water was utilized on the coolant side to dissipate the heat generated during the HFE-7100 condensation process. Utilizing a chiller (Model: ThermoChill I), the cold DI water was pumped through the wavy-channel coolant side of the condenser module. By changing the cold-water inlet temperature, various sub-cooled temperatures were achieved. The inlet/outlet temperatures of the cold water were measured using T-type thermocouples (Model: Omega TMTSS-062 U). After measuring the water mass flow rate using an ultrasonic mass flow meter with an accuracy of 0.3 % (Keyence FD-XC8M), the cold water was returned to the chiller. The pressure and temperature data were monitored and recorded with a frequency of 1 Hz by a data acquisition system (Model: KEYSIGTH 34972A). Before the condensation tests, a dry test was performed to measure the heat gain from the ambient at different condenser body temperatures. It is worth noting that the 3D-printed GRCop-42 flow condenser was hosted in a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) insulation body to minimize heat gain. The heat gain curve at different condenser body temperatures is shown in Fig. 6 .

Nominal value, range, and uncertainty of main experimental parameters for the condensation experiments are listed in Table 2 . It should be mentioned that each condensation test was repeated at least three times to ensure the repeatability of the data presented. No degradation in condensation performance or visible changes on the condenser surfaces were observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wick flow condensation model for low-surface-tension fluids
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, a semi-analytical condensation model developed to study the wick flow condensation process of a low surface-tension fluid is discussed in detail. Since the surface tension of the working fluid is low, it is assumed that a thin condensate film forms over the cold bottom surface and grows along the condenser length, as shown in Fig. 7 a. To develop the model, the following assumptions and limitations are considered:
•
Steady-state and laminar film flow regime
•
Saturated pure vapor with a uniform saturation temperature enters the condenser
•
Constant properties, as listed in Table 1, are considered for the dielectric side
•
Dielectric saturation temperature changes linearly from the inlet to the outlet
•
The wick structure in the vapor domain is at Tsat and has a negligible fin efficiency due to a high HTC associated with the condensation process
•
Vapor shear forces dominate capillary forces due to a low-surface-tension fluid being condensed
•
Subcooling of the condensate film is neglected

Fig. 7a shows a schematic of a saturated vapor stream flowing through a wick structure. As shown, the thickness of the condensate film increases along with the wick flow condenser. Since the wick-condensate thermal conductivity is higher than a pure condensate film, the heat released during the condensation process is effectively dissipated. Fig. 7b shows the balance of forces on an elemental condensate volume. The main forces are the pressure gradient in the condensate film, the viscous force, and the interfacial shear stress. The force balance on the control volume is shown in Eq. (19):

Also, the cooling water temperature increases along with the coolant flow, owing to the heat gain during the condensation process. The energy balance between the two-phase condensing side and the coolant side is considered to determine the variations in the cooling water temperature. Additionally, the dielectric saturation temperature varies due to the pressure drop. Therefore, the semi-analytical flow condensation model considers the variation of dielectric saturation temperature across the condenser module.

In this section, first, the effect of the dielectric mass flow rate on the thermo-hydraulic performances of the 3D-printed wick flow condenser is studied. Here, the wick flow condensation model is validated against the present experimental data. Then, the performance of the 3D-printed wick flow condenser at different cooling water temperatures is investigated. Finally, the effect of the wick-condensate thermal conductivity on the performance of the wick flow condenser is examined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dielectric mass flow rate on the 3D-printed wick flow condensation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the variations of condensation heat flux as a function of the dielectric mass flow rate and HTC versus exit vapor quality of the wick and plain flow condensers. The average saturation temperature of the HFE-7100 working fluid and cooling water temperature were 29.4 and 20.7 °C, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8, the flow condensation model accurately predicts the experimentally measured condensation heat flux and HTC for both wick and plain flow condensers with a maximum deviation of <5 %. It is worth noting that the experimentally measured dielectric mass flow rates and inlet/outlet subcooled temperatures are fed to the flow condensation model to predict the thermal performance. As shown in Fig. 8a, the wick flow condenser provides significantly higher condensation heat flux compared to its plain counterpart due to its unique characteristics mentioned in the concept section. The most important feature of the proposed wick flow condenser is the strategic distribution of solid-fluid interfacial area at high vapor qualities to compensate for the low thermal conductivity of the vapor phase. For example, at a dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s, the condensation heat flux increases from 8.8 kW/m2 in the plain flow condenser to 26.8 kW/m2 in the wick flow condenser, resulting in a 204.5 % improvement. Fig. 8b shows that, at a full condensation state (i.e., an exit vapor quality of 0), the converging wick structure provides a substantially higher HTC compared to that of the plain condenser. For instance, at an exit vapor quality of 0 and a dielectric mass flow rate of 0.33 g/s, the HTC of the wick flow condenser is 111 % higher than that of its plain counterpart (i.e., 2.64 versus 1.25 kW/m2-K). Also, Fig. 8b indicates that the condensation HTC increases with the dielectric mass flow rate due to the augmented convective effect at higher dielectric mass flow rates.

Fig. 9 shows the dielectric exit vapor quality and pressure drop at various dielectric mass flow rates. As shown, the semi-analytical model well predicts the experimentally measured exit vapor quality at various operating conditions with a maximum deviation of <3 %. Fig. 9a indicates that the exit vapor quality of the plain condenser decreases from 0.69 to 0 when the dielectric mass flow rate decreases from 1 to 0.25 g/s. However, as evident in Fig. 9a, the exit vapor quality of the wick flow condenser is zero (i.e., full condensation) for the examined mass flow rate range, owing to its converging wick structures augmenting the condensation heat transfer rate. Additionally, Fig. 9b shows that the experimentally measured pressure drop penalty increases with the dielectric mass flow rate. The two-phase pressure drop penalty of the wick flow condenser is higher than that of the plain flow condenser. For instance, at a dielectric mass flow rate of 0.44 g/s, the two-phase pressure drop penalty increases from 3.6 kPa in the plain flow condenser to 13.9 kPa in the wick flow condenser. This can be attributed to the lower permeability of the wick flow condenser, which increases the pressure drop penalty. Furthermore, the pressure drop penalty reduces at lower flow rates due to decreased frictional losses. Decreasing the mass flow rate from 1 to 0.33 g/s, for example, leads to a 50 % reduction in pressure drop on the wick flow condenser (i.e., from 25.5 to 12.6 kPa).

Fig. 10 shows the spatial variations of average heat flux and vapor quality versus the length of the wick flow condenser at different dielectric mass flow rates, using the wick flow condensation model. As shown in Fig. 10a, at a given mass flow rate, the condensation heat flux decreases along the condenser length due to an increase in the thickness of the condensate film. At a given condenser length, the condensation heat flux increases at higher mass flow rates due to the augmented convective heat transfer effect. For instance, the average condensation heat fluxes at the condenser outlet approach 18.3, 23, and 26.8 kW/m2 at dielectric mass flow rates of 0.33, 0.6, and 1 g/s, respectively. Simultaneously, as shown in Fig. 10b, at a given mass flow rate, the average vapor quality decreases along the condenser length, emphasizing the condensation process. Also, Fig. 10b shows that the condenser length required for full condensation (i.e., x = 0) increases at higher dielectric mass flow rates. The wick flow condensation model predicts that the full condensation occurs at the lengths of 13, 31, and 100 mm at dielectric mass flow rates of 0.33, 0.6, and 1 g/s, respectively.

Fig. 11 compares the performance evaluation factor (PEF) of the converging wick and plain flow condensers at different dielectric operating mass flow rates at a fixed dielectric saturation temperature of 29.4 °C and a fixed cooling water temperature of 20.7 °C. As shown, the PEF of the converging wick flow condenser is substantially higher than that of the converging plain flow condenser. In other words, although the converging wick flow condenser exhibits a higher pressure drop penalty than the plain flow condenser at a given dielectric mass flow rate, the high condensation heat transfer rate of the converging wick flow condenser leads to its high PEF. For instance, at a dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s, the performance evaluation factor of the conveging wick flow condenser is 69 % higher than that of the conveging plain flow condenser (i.e., a PEF of 458 vs. 271 W/W1/3). Additionally, the performance evaluation factors of both converging wick and plain flow condensers decrease at higher dielectric mass flow rates. This indicates that, at higher mass flow rates, the adverse effect of increased pressure drop penalty outweighs the beneficial impact of enhanced heat transfer rate on the PEF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of cooling water temperature on the 3D-printed wick flow condensation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 shows the variations of condensation heat flux as a function of average cooling water temperature and HTC versus exit vapor quality of the wick and plain flow condensers at a fixed dielectric saturation temperature of 31.7 °C and a fixed dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s. As shown, the proposed flow condensation model well predicts the experimentally measured heat flux and HTC results at different cooling water temperatures with a maximum deviation of <10 %. Both the model and experimental results indicate that, at a fixed dielectric mass flow rate, the condensation heat flux increases with a decrease in the cooling water temperature. For instance, the experimentally measured condensation heat flux improves by 45 % (i.e., from 24.6 to 35.7 kW/m2) when the cooling water temperature decreases from 24.9 to 12.1 °C for the wick flow condenser. This is attributed to elevated vapor pressure potentials driving the condensation process at lower water temperatures. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 12b, the model and experimental results illustrate the HTC drops by decreasing the cooling water temperature due to the formation of a thicker condensate film. For instance, the experimentally measured HTC for the wick structure flow condenser decreases from 6 to 2.5 kW/m2-K when the cooling water temperature decreases from 24.9 to 12.1 °C. With a similar trend, the HTC of the plain condenser decreases from 2.2 kW/m2-K at a cooling water temperature of 24.9 °C to 1 kW/m2-K at a cooling water temperature of 12.1 °C.

Fig. 13 illustrates the effect of cooling water temperature on the exit vapor quality and pressure drop penalty at a fixed dielectric saturation temperature of 31.7 °C and a fixed dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s. The experimental and modeling results show that decreasing the cooling water temperature leads to a reduction in the exit vapor quality. This is simply because the cooling water temperature is the main driving potential for the condensation process. The exit quality of the plain condenser, for example, decreases from 0.72 to 0.56 when the cooling water temperature decreases from 24.9 to 12.1 °C. Also, the experimental data indicate that the wick flow condenser demonstrates full condensation with an exit vapor quality of zero (i.e., a saturated or subcooled liquid state) at cooling water temperatures below 21.7 °C. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 13b, the experimentally measured pressure drop penalties of the plain and wick flow condensers remain almost constant at about 4.5 and 26 kPa, respectively. As evident, the two-phase pressure drop of the wick flow condenser is higher than that of the plain flow condenser. This highlights the need for optimization of the proposed wick flow condenser in future studies to meaningfully reduce its pressure drop penalty while maintaining a reasonably high condensation heat transfer rate.

Fig. 14 shows the spatial variations of average heat flux and vapor quality versus the length of the wick flow condenser at different cooling water temperatures, a fixed dielectric saturation temperature of 31.7 °C, and a fixed dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s. As shown in Fig. 14a, at a fixed cooling water temperature, the local heat flux decreases along the condenser length due to the growth in the condensate film thickness. For instance, at a cooling water temperature of 12.1 °C, the average heat flux decreases from 90 kW/m2 at a condenser length of 20 mm to 37 kW/m2 at a condenser length of 100 mm. It is also clear that the local heat flux increases at lower cooling water temperatures due to the higher temperature difference driving the condensation process. For example, at a condenser length of 20 mm, the average heat flux increases from 55 to 90 kW/m2 when the cooling water temperature decreases from 24.9 to 12.1 °C. Additionally, Fig. 14b shows that the condenser length required for full condensation decreases at lower cooling water temperatures due to the amplified temperature driving forces. For instance, the condenser length required for full condensation decreases from 100 to 36 mm when the cooling water temperature decreases from 21.7 to 12.1 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of effective wick-condensate thermal conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: Upon validation of the developed flow condensation model against the experimental results, the model is employed to predict the condensation heat flux and exit vapor quality of the wick flow condenser at extended working conditions beyond the experimental range of the current study. Fig. 15 a shows the condensation heat flux as a function of the cooling water temperature at different effective wick-condensate thermal conductivities and a fixed dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s. From an experimental standpoint, the effective wick-condensate thermal conductivity can be adjusted by changing pore size, condenser material, and working fluid. Here, the effective wick-condensate thermal conductivities of 0.43, 0.63, and 0.83 W/m-K are examined. At a fixed cooling water temperature, the condensation heat flux increases as the effective wick-condensate thermal conductivity increases. For example, at a dielectric saturation temperature of 23 °C and a cooling water temperature of 17 °C, the condensation heat flux of the wick flow condenser increases from 12.1 to 15.9 and 19.4 when the effective wick-condensate thermal conductivity increases from 0.43 to 0.63 and 0.83 W/m-K, respectively. As the condensation heat flux increases with the effective wick-condensate thermal conductivity, the exit vapor quality decreases, as shown in Fig. 15b For instance, at a cooling water temperature of 17 °C, the exit vapor quality of the wick flow condenser decreases from 0.55 to 0.4 and 0.27 when the effective wick-condensate thermal conductivity increases from 0.43 to 0.63 and 0.83 W/m-K, respectively. This is attributed to the temperature of the liquid-vapor interface, which is cooled more effectively at higher wick-condensate thermal conductivities.

In summary, a novel 3D-printed converging wick flow condenser made of GRCop-42 copper alloy was introduced and examined using HFE-7100 dielectric as a representative low surface tension fluid. The thermo-hydraulic performance of the converging wick flow condenser was benchmarked against a plain flow condenser using both experimental and modeling approaches. The experimental and modeling results indicated that the condensation heat flux increased from 8.8 kW/m2 in the plain flow condenser to 26.8 kW/m2 in the wick flow condenser at a dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s, resulting in a 204.5 % improvement. Also, at a full condensation state (i.e., an exit vapor quality of 0) and a dielectric mass flow rate of 0.33 g/s, the condensation HTC of the wick condenser showed a 111 % improvement compared to its plain condenser counterpart. Additionally, the results indicate that, at a dielectric saturation temperature of 29.4 °C and a cooling water temperature of 20.7 °C, the performance evaluation factor of the conveging wick flow condenser is 69 % higher than that of the conveging plain flow condenser. Furthermore, at a dielectric mass flow rate of 1 g/s, a dielectric saturation temperature of 23 °C, and a cooling water temperature of 14 °C, the validated wick flow condensation model predicted exit vapor qualities of about 0.4, 0.2, and 0 (i.e., full condensation) at effective wick-condensate thermal conductivities of 0.43, 0.63, and 0.83 W/m-K, respectively. The findings of the present study lay the foundation for the design of innovative 3D-printed wick flow structures for enhanced flow condensation of extremely wetting low-surface-tension fluids.
